<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243966647 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 06548: User Interface Modeling

- page_id: `243966647`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243966647/User+Interface+Modeling
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:50:07.389+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Diagrams > Extension diagrams > User Interface Modeling diagram
- labels: ['unrestored-unknown-attachment', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

34598273

INLINE

534534873

INLINE

3459873459

INLINE

**On this Page:**

**5**

34583045879

INLINE

This section presents basic information about User Interface Modeling in a modeling tool.

##### Modifying a table

As the following figure shows, the newly created table has two columns and two rows already added to the table. Each row contains a number of cells equal to the number of columns.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Structure of newly created table

To modify the contents of the table, you can do the following:

- Change the titles of the existing columns.
- Add a new column.
- Change the order of the columns.
- Add a new row.
- Change the order of the rows.
- Add cell values.

To add a new column to a table

1. Right-click the table in the Containment tree. From the shortcut menu, select Create Element > Column . A new column is added to the table.
2. Type the title for the new column. With the new column, a new cell is added to each row of the table.

To change the order of the columns in a table

1. Right-click a column of the table in the Containment tree. From the shortcut menu, select Element Numbering . The Element Numbering dialog opens.
2. In the list on the right side of the dialog, select all columns and click Create . To select all columns, click the first column in the list and then hold down the Shift key when selecting the last one. [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2017-07-15 at 4.08.46 PM.png']
3. All the columns become numbered. [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2017-07-15 at 4.10.52 PM.png']
4. Change the order of columns in the table by changing their positions in the list (see the following figure). The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Element Numbering dialog' space=''] feature is to order the selected items.
  - To move a column to the left, select the column and click the Decrease button.
  - To move a column to the right, select the column and click the Increase button.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Moving column "Team" to left

To add a row to a table

1. Right-click the table in the Containment tree. From the shortcut menu, select Create Element > Row . A new row is added to the table.
2. Type the title for the new row.

To change the order of the rows in a table

1. Right-click a row of the table in the Containment tree. From the shortcut menu, select Element Numbering . The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Element Numbering dialog' space='']**Element Numbering** dialog opens.
2. In the list on the right side of the dialog, select all rows and click Create .
  - To select all rows, click the first row in the list and then hold down the Shift key when selecting the last one. All the rows become numbered.
3. Change the order of rows in the table by changing their positions in the list.
  - To move a row up, select the row and click the Decrease button.
  - To move a row down, select the row and click the Increase button.

To change a cell value

1. Select the cell of the table row in the Containment tree.
2. Change the cell value in one of the following ways:
  - Press the F2 key. Type a new value.
  - Click the cell value again and wait while it becomes editable. Type a new value. Press Enter when you are done.
  - Right-click the cell to open the Specification window of the cell. Type a new value in the cell of the Text property value.

##### Modifying a tree

You can add as many nodes and leaves to a tree as you need. You can also change the order of the elements in the tree.

To add a node or a leaf to a tree or to another node

1. Right-click the tree or a node in the Containment tree. From the shortcut menu, select Create Element > Node or Leaf .
2. Type a name for the new node or leaf.

To change the order of the elements in a tree

At a time, you can rearrange the order only of the same parent- node leaves and/or nodes.

1. Right-click a leaf or a node of the tree. From the shortcut menu select Element Numbering . The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Element Numbering dialog' space='']**Element Numbering** dialog opens.
2. In the list on the right side of the dialog, select all leaves and/or nodes, and then click Create .
  - To select all leaves and/or nodes, click the first leaf or node in the list and then hold down the Shift key when selecting the last leaf or node in the list.
3. All the leaves and/or nodes become numbered.
4. Change the order of leaves and/or nodes in the tree by changing their positions in the list appropriately.
  - To move a leaf or a node/leaf up, select the row and click the Decrease button.
  - To move a leaf or a node/leaf down, select the row and click the Increase button.

##### Nesting

You can move and arrange User Interface Modeling elements since they nest each other, and create deep structured User Interface models. However, not all elements have the nesting ability: only **Container** elements can nest other elements. All frames nest in each other. As a result, if you were to move the element with title *Frame 1*, all other elements would stay in position within this element.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of a deep structure that was created using containers

##### Reusability

If you have created a complex model and need to use it again, you do not have to create a new one. All you need to do is select the elements, copy them and paste them into the same diagram or in any other diagram. You can also reuse a single element by copying and pasting it or by dragging it from the Browser onto the diagram pane.

##### Specifying elements

You can modify UI modeling elements by editing their properties in the following three ways:

- Via the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space=''] .
- Via the shortcut menu.
- On a diagram pane.

To edit properties via the Specification window

1. Select an element on the diagram.
2. Right-click on it and select Specification on the shortcut menu.
3. Edit one of the properties to modify the element.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Specification dialog box of the Slider element

The so-called boolean properties that have only two values can also be edited via the shortcut menu.

The following instructions are for boolean properties ONLY.

To edit properties via the shortcut menu

1. Select an element on the diagram.
2. Right-click it. The shortcut menu will appear and the editable properties will be displayed at the bottom.
3. Select the property you want to change.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Shortcut menu of the Slider element with boolean properties

For the elements that own a text or a title (such as a button or a frame), it is also possible to edit the appropriate properties straight on a diagram.

The following instructions are for the text or title properties ONLY.

To edit the element properties on a diagram

1. Select an element on the diagram.
2. Click the selected element.
3. Type a text on the element (see the following figure).
4. Press ENTER. The text you have typed will appear on the element and in the Text / Title property of the element as well.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Editing title of element

##### Icon usage

Some elements are capable of owning an icon. User Interface Modeling provides a number of frequently-used icons, such as cut, delete, undo/redo, etc. Of course, it is also possible to use images from your computer as icons.

To add an icon

1. Select an element that can own an icon, such as a button.
2. Right-click the element and select Specification in the shortcut menu.
3. In the Icon list, select the icon you want to appear on the element.
  - If you want to use an image from your computer as an icon, click custom .

##### Using symbol properties

User Interface Modeling also allows you to modify User Interface elements via [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space=''].

To edit an element via symbol properties

1. Select an element on the diagram.
2. Right-click and select Symbol Properties on the shortcut menu.
3. Change one of the properties listed in the table below. For example, if you want to set the same background color to more than one UI modeling element, you can select all these elements in the diagram and then change the required property for all of them in the same way.

The following table lists the supported symbol properties:

| Symbol Property | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Fill Color | Sets the background color of the component to the chosen color; the property Use Fill Color must be marked. |
| Font | Sets the font if there is any text in the component. |
| Text Color | Sets the text color of the component to the chosen color. |
| Pen Color | Sets the color of the border for components that have a color. |

##### User interface prototyping

User Interface Modeling allows you to create browsable reports. You can create a hyperlink from the UI model element to the report. Once a report of these models has been created, click on any element in the report. You will then be directed to the other diagram. When you click **OK** in the upper left-hand frame you will be directed to the next frame. After clicking **OK** in that frame, you can get to a couple of frames from which you can step forward and backward as you want, as they are all linked to one another.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Case Studies for User Interface Modeling' space='']provides an example of how this feature works and explains how to add hyperlinks and create browsable reports.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of User Interface Models and their Workflow

3452345879

INLINE

**Related References:**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML diagrams' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Extension diagrams' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='User Interface Modeling diagram' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d161cbe2-082d-42e9-a5a2-26b81fbe412f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">34598273</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6657cd03-7ce7-4a7d-8cd7-a99ab4af710c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">534534873</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="04e6a23b-7761-4859-85d3-577aea17ed7e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">3459873459</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this Page:</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="729a0e1f-22f0-4461-b59b-ee229aa7f856"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">5</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro><br /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d64dee7f-a0c9-4c30-92ec-d3050481d3c9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">34583045879</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>This section presents basic information about User Interface Modeling in a modeling tool.</p><h3>Modifying a table</h3><p>As the following figure shows, the newly created table has two columns and two rows already added to the table. Each row contains a number of cells equal to the number of columns. </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="345" ac:width="291"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddava464785eb4fe74b8074a443685626c52.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Structure of newly created table</h6><p>To modify the contents of the table, you can do the following: </p><ul><li>Change the titles of the existing columns. </li><li>Add a new column. </li><li>Change the order of the columns. </li><li>Add a new row. </li><li>Change the order of the rows. </li><li>Add cell values.</li></ul><p><br /></p><p>To add a new column to a table </p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the table in the Containment tree. From the shortcut menu, select <strong>Create </strong> <strong>Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Column</strong>. A new column is added to the table.</li><li>Type the title for the new column. With the new column, a new cell is added to each row of the table.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To change the order of the columns in a table</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click a column of the table in the Containment tree. From the shortcut menu, select <strong>Element Numbering</strong>. The <strong>Element Numbering</strong> dialog opens.</li><li>In the list on the right side of the dialog, select all columns and click <strong>Create</strong>. To select all columns, click the first column in the list and then hold down the Shift key when selecting the last one.<br /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="700"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Screen Shot 2017-07-15 at 4.08.46 PM.png" /></ac:image></li><li>All the columns become numbered.<br /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="700"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Screen Shot 2017-07-15 at 4.10.52 PM.png" /></ac:image></li><li>Change the order of columns in the table by changing their positions in the list (see the following figure). The <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Element Numbering dialog" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Element Numbering ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>feature is to order the selected items.<ul><li>To move a column to the left, select the column and click the <strong>Decrease</strong> button.</li><li>To move a column to the right, select the column and click the <strong>Increase</strong> button.</li></ul></li></ol><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="435" ac:width="480"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav0f644ec5c62393ebf866385909b0158e.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Moving column &quot;Team&quot; to left</h6><p>To add a row to a table</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the table in the Containment tree. From the shortcut menu, select <strong>Create </strong><strong>Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Row</strong>. A new row is added to the table.</li><li>Type the title for the new row.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To change the order of the rows in a table</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click a row of the table in the Containment tree. From the shortcut menu, select <strong>Element Numbering</strong>. The <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Element Numbering dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Element Numbering</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link> opens.</li><li>In the list on the right side of the dialog, select all rows and click <strong>Create</strong>.<ul><li>To select all rows, click the first row in the list and then hold down the Shift key when selecting the last one. All the rows become numbered. </li></ul></li><li>Change the order of rows in the table by changing their positions in the list.<ul><li>To move a row up, select the row and click the <strong>Decrease</strong> button. </li><li>To move a row down, select the row and click the <strong>Increase</strong> button.</li></ul></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To change a cell value</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the cell of the table row in the Containment tree. </li><li>Change the cell value in one of the following ways:<ul><li>Press the F2 key. Type a new value. </li><li>Click the cell value again and wait while it becomes editable. Type a new value. Press Enter when you are done. </li><li>Right-click the cell to open the Specification window of the cell. Type a new value in the cell of the <strong>Text</strong> property value. </li></ul></li></ol><h3>Modifying a tree</h3><p>You can add as many nodes and leaves to a tree as you need. You can also change the order of the elements in the tree.</p><p><br /></p><p>To add a node or a leaf to a tree or to another node</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the tree or a node in the Containment tree. From the shortcut menu, select <strong>Create Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Node</strong> or <strong>Leaf</strong>.</li><li>Type a name for the new node or leaf.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To change the order of the elements in a tree</p><hr /><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="aee00063-d6e1-45a9-921e-e919e2ebb29c"><ac:rich-text-body><p>At a time, you can rearrange the order only of the same parent- node leaves and/or nodes.</p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="220" ac:width="322"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav4fa229efa259b3b59f544c82cc4d8d07.png" /></ac:image></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ol><li>Right-click a leaf or a node of the tree. From the shortcut menu select <strong>Element</strong><strong>Numbering</strong>. The <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Element Numbering dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Element Numbering</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link> opens.</li><li>In the list on the right side of the dialog, select all leaves and/or nodes, and then click <strong>Create</strong>.<ul><li>To select all leaves and/or nodes, click the first leaf or node in the list and then hold down the Shift key when selecting the last leaf or node in the list.</li></ul></li><li>All the leaves and/or nodes become numbered.</li><li>Change the order of leaves and/or nodes in the tree by changing their positions in the list appropriately.<ul><li>To move a leaf or a node/leaf up, select the row and click the <strong>Decrease</strong> button. </li><li>To move a leaf or a node/leaf down, select the row and click the <strong>Increase</strong> button.</li></ul></li></ol><h3>Nesting</h3><p>You can move and arrange User Interface Modeling elements since they nest each other, and create deep structured User Interface models. However, not all elements have the nesting ability: only <strong>Container</strong> elements can nest other elements. All frames nest in each other. As a result, if you were to move the element with title <em>Frame 1</em>, all other elements would stay in position within this element. </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="359" ac:width="464"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavb48f115e0ff673ee99f82c420118b5a6.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of a deep structure that was created using containers</h6><h3>Reusability</h3><p>If you have created a complex model and need to use it again, you do not have to create a new one. All you need to do is select the elements, copy them and paste them into the same diagram or in any other diagram. You can also reuse a single element by copying and pasting it or by dragging it from the Browser onto the diagram pane. </p><h3>Specifying elements</h3><p>You can modify UI modeling elements by editing their properties in the following three ways: </p><ul><li>Via the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Specification window]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </li><li>Via the shortcut menu. </li><li>On a diagram pane.</li></ul><p><br /></p><p>To edit properties via the Specification window</p><hr /><ol><li>Select an element on the diagram.</li><li>Right-click on it and select <strong>Specification</strong> on the shortcut menu.</li><li>Edit one of the properties to modify the element.</li></ol><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="443" ac:width="544"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddava5e1fce994dba1b3b20391944e4f227f.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Specification dialog box of the Slider element</h6><p>The so-called boolean properties that have only two values can also be edited via the shortcut menu. </p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="999b5aad-6e80-4040-904c-9b973e57e015"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The following instructions are for boolean properties ONLY. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To edit properties via the shortcut menu</p><hr /><ol><li>Select an element on the diagram.</li><li>Right-click it. The shortcut menu will appear and the editable properties will be displayed at the bottom.</li><li>Select the property you want to change. </li></ol><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="322" ac:width="398"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavbffe5c4d37f6f962fdc3946caf66d50e.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Shortcut menu of the Slider element with boolean properties</h6><p>For the elements that own a text or a title (such as a button or a frame), it is also possible to edit the appropriate properties straight on a diagram.</p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fcb05e07-0728-48cb-8a1c-493942d052e1"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The following instructions are for the text or title properties ONLY.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To edit the element properties on a diagram</p><hr /><ol><li>Select an element on the diagram.</li><li>Click the selected element.</li><li>Type a text on the element (see the following figure).</li><li>Press ENTER. The text you have typed will appear on the element and in the <strong>Text</strong>/<strong>Title </strong>property of the element as well.</li></ol><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="47" ac:width="120"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav11240983e48d0b0469795a548c6e0311.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Editing title of element</h6><h3>Icon usage</h3><p>Some elements are capable of owning an icon. User Interface Modeling provides a number of frequently-used icons, such as cut, delete, undo/redo, etc. Of course, it is also possible to use images from your computer as icons.</p><p><br /></p><p>To add an icon</p><hr /><ol><li>Select an element that can own an icon, such as a button.</li><li>Right-click the element and select <strong>Specification</strong> in the shortcut menu. </li><li>In the <strong>Icon</strong> list, select the icon you want to appear on the element.<ul><li>If you want to use an image from your computer as an icon, click <strong>custom</strong>. </li></ul></li></ol><h3 style="text-align: left;">Using symbol properties</h3><p>User Interface Modeling also allows you to modify User Interface elements via <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[symbol properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><br /></p><p>To edit an element via symbol properties</p><hr /><ol><li>Select an element on the diagram.</li><li>Right-click and select <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> on the shortcut menu.</li><li>Change one of the properties listed in the table below. For example, if you want to set the same background color to more than one UI modeling element, you can select all these elements in the diagram and then change the required property for all of them in the same way.</li></ol><p>The following table lists the supported symbol properties:</p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Symbol Property</strong></p></td><td><p><strong>Effect</strong></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Fill Color</p></td><td><p>Sets the background color of the component to the chosen color; the property <strong>Use Fill Color</strong> must be marked.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Font</p></td><td><p>Sets the font if there is any text in the component.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Text Color</p></td><td><p>Sets the text color of the component to the chosen color.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Pen Color</p></td><td><p>Sets the color of the border for components that have a color.</p></td></tr></tbody></table><h3>User interface prototyping</h3><p>User Interface Modeling allows you to create browsable reports. You can create a hyperlink from the UI model element to the report. Once a report of these models has been created, click on any element in the report. You will then be directed to the other diagram. When you click <strong>OK</strong> in the upper left-hand frame you will be directed to the next frame. After clicking <strong>OK</strong> in that frame, you can get to a couple of frames from which you can step forward and backward as you want, as they are all linked to one another.</p><p><span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ac:link> </span>provides an example of how this feature works and explains how to add hyperlinks and create browsable reports.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="UI_workflow.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);">Example of User Interface Models and their Workflow</span></h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ef3c7dc6-0bea-45f6-825c-81e2a9b44441"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">3452345879</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related References:</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="UML diagrams" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Extension diagrams" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Extension diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="6521fee3-8b99-486c-b414-30a47e565157"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="User Interface Modeling diagram" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243966634 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 06549: User Interface Modeling diagram

- page_id: `243966634`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243966634/User+Interface+Modeling+diagram
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-07T17:22:39.074+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Diagrams > Extension diagrams
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1192686252

INLINE

100.00002%

262346576

INLINE

234523468

INLINE

A User Interface (UI) Modeling diagram makes it possible to build prototypes of user interfaces, connect UI mockups with the whole Architectural model, export them as images, and create browsable reports for presentations. In short, they help gather information faster and thus save time and money.

The advantage of User Interface Modeling lies in its ability to:

- Create WYSIWYG User Interface prototypes rapidly.
- Integrate User Interface development with UML specifications.
- Get immediate feedback from prospective users on real situations and reuse it for future designs.
- Create browsable reports with the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Report Wizard' space=''].

****

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of User Interface diagram Model for *Calculator*

****

373920617

**Related pages**

- UML diagrams
- Extension diagram

****

**Sample m****odel**

A Web UI sample project, representing the UI Modeling Diagram usage for web-based interface modeling, is available at <*modeling tool**installation directory>\samples\diagrams\User* Interface Modeling.

rapidly.</li><li>Integrate User Interface development with UML specifications.</li><li>Get immediate feedback from prospective users on real situations and reuse it for future designs.</li><li>Create browsable reports with the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Report Wizard" /></ac:link>.</strong></li></ul><p><strong><br /></strong></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ## PAGE 06550: User Task

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1486336455

1486336456

1486336455

**Description**

A User Task is a typical workflow task where a human performer performs the task with the assistance of software and is scheduled through a task list manager of some sort.

**Notation**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

1486336454

**Related elements**

- Activities
- Task
- Service Task
- Send Task
- Receive Task
- Manual Task
- Business Rule Task
- Script Task

- BPMN Collaboration Diagram

**Related procedure**

- Creating and Using Tasks

****

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249567004 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 06551: Using Activities

- page_id: `249567004`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249567004/Using+Activities
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-10T13:30:04.168+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > BPMN modeling > Using Cameo Business Modeler Plugin > Using BPMN Process Diagram
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This section will describe how to use Activities in a BPMN process diagram. Activities (Tasks, Subprocesses, and Call Activities) can have the following specific markers displayed on their shapes:

- Compensation
- Standard loop
- MultiInstance loop (parallel)
- MultiInstance loop (sequential)

To add a Compensation marker to an Activity, do one of the following

- Right-click an Activity and select Is For Compensation .
- Open the Activity Specification window. Click to clear the Is For Compensation check box and click Close .

To add a Loop marker to an Activity

- Right-click an Activity and click to select Standard Loop or MultiInstance Loop .

To display a MultiInstance Loop (sequential) marker on a MultiInstance Loop activity, do one of the following

- Right-click an Activity with a MultiInstance Loop (parallel) marker and on the shortcut menu select Is Sequential .
- Open the Activity with a MultiInstance marker Specification window. Select Is Sequential and click Close .

To convert an Activity to another type of Activity

- Right-click a Task, SubProcess, or Call Activity and from the shortcut menu select Refactor > Convert To and then select an Activity type to which you need to convert the selected Activity.

To display Resources assigned for Activities

- Right-click the diagram pane and then do one of the following:
- On the shortcut menu, click to select the Show Assigned Resources check box (see the following figure).
- From the shortcut menu, select Diagram Properties and in the open dialog, set the Show Assigned Resources property value to true.

**Related element**

- Activities

**Related diagrams**

- BPMN Process Diagram
- BPMN Collaboration Diagram

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='']
- Creating and Using Tasks
- Creating and Using SubProcesses
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating and Using a BPMN Event' space='']
- Creating and Using a Sequence Flow
- Creating and Using Data Items
- Using Common BPMN Elements
- Navigation Between BPMN Diagrams

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249561351 space=MT version=5 -->
## PAGE 06552: Using Association Block as Connector type

- page_id: `249561351`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249561351/Using+Association+Block+as+Connector+type
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2025-09-09T12:10:37.044+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Modeling structure with Blocks > Connecting Blocks in SysML Internal Block Diagram
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association Block' space='MED'] can relate two [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='MED']together, as well as having an internal structure and other features of its own. The purpose of the Connector Property is to show that internal structure and features using [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML v1 specific compartments' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Property path notation' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying internal structures on structured classifiers' space=''], or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements' space='']. You can specify more detail for [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MED'] by typing them with Association Blocks. The Connector Property is created in the model after the Connector is typed by an Association Block. The Connector Property allows representation of Association Block usages in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space=''].

To use the Association Block as a Connector type

1. Set the Association Block as a Connector type in one of the following ways: - In the Containment tree, select Association Block and drag it onto a Connector on the diagram pane. - In the Containment tree, select Association Block and drag it onto a Connector on the diagram pane. H old it down until the Tooltip appears (long drag) and drop it. From the opened menu, select the **Set as Type** command.
2. Select the Connector Property, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying parts and ports in SysML v1' space='']open the **Display Parts/Ports** dialog , and select internal structure fragments of Association Block to show them on the Connector Property shape. The internal structure is shown inside the Connector Property shape. See the figure below for an example.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Connector Property shows the internal structure of the *Water Delivery* Association Block and is connected with the *Diameter* Constraint Property.

**Related pages**

- Creating Association Block

**Sample model**

The model used in the figure of this page is the **WaterSupply_19.0** sample model. Download[ATTACHMENT filename='WaterSupply_19.0.mdzip']*.*

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

See the figure below for an example.</li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;"><br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="connectorProperty_in_ibd.png" /></ac:image><br />The Connector Property shows the internal structure of the <em>Water Delivery</em> Association Block and is connected with the <em>Diameter</em> Constraint Property.</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="7e2d8f6e-924b-47b6-9983-99429ebefffb"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Defining+Blocks+in+Block+Definition+Diagram#DefiningBlocksinBlockDefinitionDiagram-CreatingAssociationBlock">Creating Association Block</a></li></ul></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Sample model</strong></p><p>The model used in the figure of this page is the <strong>WaterSupply_19.0</strong> sample model. Download<span class="confluence-link"> <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="WaterSupply_19.0.mdzip" /></ac:link><em><span>.</span></em></span></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249565362 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 06555: Using BPMN Compliance Mode

- page_id: `249565362`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249565362/Using+BPMN+Compliance+Mode
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:07:46.118+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > Procedures
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

BPMN elements can be integrated into the UAF model. The Processes (BPD) and Sequences (BPD) diagrams in the UAF model are based on BPMN Process Diagram (BPD).Some diagram elements have both UAF and BPMN stereotypes.

BPMN toolbars are available in the following diagrams:

| Framework | Diagrams |
| --- | --- |
| UAF 1.3 | Operational Process Flow (BPD) |
| DoDAF 2.1 |  |
| MODAF | MODAF. OV-5 Operational Activity Model BPDMODAF. OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description BPDMODAF. SV-4 Functionality Description BPDMODAF. SV-10c Resource Event-Trace Description BPD |
| NAF | NOV-5 Operational Activity Model BPDNOV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description BPDNSV-4 System Functionality Description BPDNSV-10c System Event-Trace Description BPD |
| NAF 4.0 | L4 Logical Activities BPDL6 Logical Sequence BPDP4 Resource Functions BPDP6 Resource Sequence BPD |

There are two ways to turn the BPMN compliance mode on:

- Creating a new project. In this case, BPMN compliance affects all UAF elements compatible with BPMN.
- Change the Project Options for the existing project. In this case, you can choose if you want the BPMN compliance to affect only new or existing elements.

You will also be suggested to turn the BPMN compliance on while creating the BPD diagram.

To turn on the BPMN compliance mode

1. Click Options > Project .
2. In the Project Options dialog, click the UAF property group.
3. Set the BPMN Compliance Mode property value to true.
4. Click OK .

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

###### **Mapping UAF and BPMN**

The mapping list of UAF and BPMN stereotypes:

| UAF | BPMN |
| --- | --- |
| Operational Activity Action | Call Activity |
| Function Action | Call Activity |
| Operational Activity | BPMN Process |
| Function | BPMN Process |
| Service Function | BPMN Process |
| Operational Activity Edge [Control Flow] | Sequence Flow |
| Function Edge [Control Flow] | Sequence Flow |
| Operational Activity Edge [Object Flow] | Data Association |
| Function Edge [Object Flow] | Data Association |
| Operational Agent | Resource |
| Resource Performer | Resource |
| Operational Information | BPMN Message |
| Resource Information | BPMN Message |

###### **Example**

Let's see an example of how can BPMN Process elements be used in an Operational Process Flow (BPD) diagram.

In the created Operational Process Flow (BPD) diagram, the Claim Processing behavior was modeled using the BPMN Process elements. The following figure shows the BPMN Process diagram, where some elements have both UAF and BPMN stereotypes.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

In this case, BPMN compliance affects <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="d7580b35-30b2-4d8b-b03b-dd07e9a519b9">all UAF elements</ac:inline-comment-marker> compatible with BPMN.</li><li>Change the Project Options for the existing project. In this case, you can choose if you want the BPMN compliance to affect only new or existing elements.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c56a2636-9c30-4889-b81a-7e5ccb131b40"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You will also be suggested to turn the BPMN compliance on while creating the BPD diagram.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="43719ce5-0d71-4f64-8b84-d97180955565">To turn on the BPMN</ac:inline-comment-marker> compliance mode </p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Project</strong>.</li><li>In the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog, click the <strong>UAF </strong>property group.</li><li>Set the <strong>BPMN Compliance Mode</strong> property value to true.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol><p><strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2023-3-28_14-46-9.png" /></ac:image><br /></strong></p><p><br /></p><h4><strong>Mapping UAF and BPMN</strong></h4><p style="text-align: left;">The mapping list of UAF and BPMN stereotypes:</p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>UAF</th><th>BPMN</th></tr><tr><td>Operational Activity Action</td><td>Call Activity</td></tr><tr><td>Function Action</td><td>Call Activity</td></tr><tr><td>Operational Activity</td><td>BPMN Process</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">Function </td><td colspan="1">BPMN Process</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">Service Function</td><td colspan="1">BPMN Process</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">Operational Activity Edge [Control Flow]</td><td colspan="1">Sequence Flow</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">Function Edge [Control Flow]</td><td colspan="1">Sequence Flow</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">Operational Activity Edge [Object Flow]</td><td colspan="1">Data Association</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">Function Edge [Object Flow]</td><td colspan="1">Data Association</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">Operational Agent</td><td colspan="1">Resource</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">Resource Performer</td><td colspan="1">Resource</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">Operational Information</td><td colspan="1">BPMN Message</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">Resource Information</td><td colspan="1">BPMN Message</td></tr></tbody></table><h4><strong>Example</strong></h4><p>Let's see an example of how can BPMN Process elements be used in an Operational Process Flow (BPD) diagram.</p><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">In the created Operational Process Flow (BPD) diagram, the Claim Processing behavior was modeled using <span>the BPMN Process elements</span>. The following figure shows</span> the BPMN Process diagram, where some elements have both UAF and BPMN stereotypes. </p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2023-3-28_15-13-44.png" /></ac:image></p><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249567047 space=MT version=4 -->
## PAGE 06556: Using BPMN Element Numbers

- page_id: `249567047`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249567047/Using+BPMN+Element+Numbers
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-09-10T13:09:26.237+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > BPMN modeling > Using Cameo Business Modeler Plugin
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Most BPMN elements can have specified their IDs. Element ID is automatically created for BPMN Process, Collaboration, and Choreography diagram flow elements and conversations. The element IDs are displayed (in gray) in the following places:

- On a diagram. The ID is displayed above or before the name of element.
- In the Model Browser. The ID is displayed in front of the name of element.
- In the Specification window. The ID is below the Name property.

Automatic elements numbering is turned off in all the projects that are created usingBPMN2 Project and BPMN-SoaML Project templates.

To specify or change a BPMN element’s ID manually

1. Open the Specification window of the element.
2. Type a number in the Id property value box.

To turn on/off automatic elements numbering in a project

1. On the main menu, click Options > Project .
2. In the Project Options dialog, click General project options in the options list on the left.
3. Click to clear the Use Element Auto-numbering check box.
4. Click OK .

To hide an element Id on a diagram

- Right-click the diagram pane and then do one of the following:
  - On the shortcut menu clear the Show Elements Id .
  - From the shortcut menu, select Diagram Properties . Clear the Show Elements Id check box.

To hide element numbers in the Containment tree

1. On the main menu, click Options > Project .
2. In the Project Options dialog, click General project options in the options list on the left.
3. Click to clear the Display Element Number check box.
4. Click OK .

To edit elements numbering

1. Right-click an element on the BPMN2 diagram and select Element Numbering from the shortcut menu. The Element Numberin g dialog opens.
2. Change elements numbers into desired ones at the right side in this dialog.
3. Click OK .

To change displayed numbering style for element symbols on a diagram

1. Right-click an element on the BPMN2 diagram and select Symbol(s) Properties .
2. In the Symbol Properties dialog, select the Element Number Display Mode property value from the drop-down list.
3. Select the Show Number Tag Name check box to show prefix id = before an element number.

**Related element**

- Numbering Elements

**Related diagrams**

- BPMN Process Diagram
- BPMN Collaboration Diagram
- BPMN Choreography Diagram

**Related external resource**

- Generic numbering mechanism
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Numbering Customizations' space='UPDG']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249567031 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 06560: Using Choreography Activity

- page_id: `249567031`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249567031/Using+Choreography+Activity
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-10T13:12:24.684+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > BPMN modeling > Using Cameo Business Modeler Plugin > Using BPMN Choreography Diagram
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This section describes how to create and model choreography activities in a BPMN Choreography diagram. You can specify a list of Participants in a Choreography Activity from

- Specification window
- Smart Manipulator toolbar
- Drag-and-drop operation

To specify a list of Participants from the Specification window

1. Do one of the following:
  - Select the Choreography Activity on the diagram pane and on the Smart Manipulator toolbar, click the Participants button.
  - Open the Specification window, click the Participants property value cell and then click ... button.
2. In the open Select Recourse dialog, select Resources.
3. Click OK .

You need to specify at least two Participants for a Choreography Activity.

The MultiInstance Participants on a Choreography shape will be displayed with a MultiInstance marker in the Participants’ compartment.

To specify a list of participants from the drag-and-drop action

- Drag a Resource from the Containment tree to a Choreography Activity on diagram pane.

- Hold down Shift to select multiple elements that are grouped together.
- Hold down Ctrl to select multiple elements that are not grouped together.

To specify an Initiating Participant in a Choreography Activity

Initiating Participant

1. Open the Choreography Activity Specification window.
2. Click the Initiating Participant property value cell and select a Resource.

To add a Loop marker to a Choreography Activity

- Right-click a Choreography Activity and select Standard Loop or MultiInstance Loop .

To display a MultiInsatance Loop (sequential) marker on a MultiInstane Loop Choreography Activity, do one of the following

- Right-click a Choreography Activity with the MultiInstance Loop (parallel) marker and select Is Sequential .
- Open the Choreography Activity with the MultiInstance Loop marker Specification window. Select the Is Sequential check box.

**Related elements**

- Choreography Activities
- Participant
- Resource

**Related diagram**

- BPMN Choreography Diagram

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='']
- Creating and Using Choreography Task
- Creating SubChoreography
- Creating Call Choreography Activity
- Creating and Using Participant
- Using Common BPMN Elements

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243973848 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 06561: Using code completion to develop BeanShell scripts

- page_id: `243973848`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243973848/Using+code+completion+to+develop+BeanShell+scripts
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T19:18:22.211+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Macro engine > Supported scripting languages
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can use any text editor to develop a scripting language. However, a standard text editor lacks of code assistant features. Most scripting languages are loose type. For example, to define a variable in JavaScript, you need to type:

```text

```

It is difficult to determine what type of "a" later in the source code. With BeanShell, you can use a variable without declaring it, for example:

```text

```

Or you can declare it first:

```text

```

Java IDE does not officially support code completion for scripting languages. However, there is a workaround if you use BeanShell. First you need a Java IDE. If you do not have one, you can select NetBeans because it has the smallest file size. You can download the latest version of NetBeans at *[http://www.netbeans.org/downloads/index.html](http://www.netbeans.org/downloads/index.html)*. The NetBeans Java SE package is enough. Second you need to set up a MagicDraw classpath.

To set up a classpath point in the MagicDraw library in NetBeans

1. Click Tools > Libraries on the main menu. The Library Manager dialog will open.
2. Click the New Library button. The New Library dialog will open.
3. Specify a library name, for example, MD16.6. The Library type must be Class Libraries .
4. Click OK to close the New Library dialog.
5. Select your new library in the Libraries tree.
6. Click the Add JAR/Folder button and add all the JAR files in <MagicDraw>/lib .
7. Click OK to close the Library Manager dialog.

To develop a BeanShell script in NetBeans

1. Click File > New Project on the main menu to create a Java application project. The New Project dialog will open.
2. Select Java in the Categories box and Java Application in the Projects box, and then follow the instructions.
3. Expand your project node in the Project window. The Libraries node will appear.
4. Right-click the Libraries node and select Add Library from the shortcut menu.
5. Select the MagicDraw library that you have previously created (see "To set up a classpath point in the MagicDraw library in NetBeans:") and click Add Library .
6. Click File > New File on the main menu to add a new Java file.
7. Select Java in the Categories box and Empty Java File in the File Types box, and then follow the instructions until finish.

You need to create a public static method in a Java file, for example, main() method, to follow the standard Java programming language. At the end of the file, insert a statement to call the static method. See the example in *create_project_elements.bsh* in the *<MagicDraw>/samples/product features/macros* directory.

The official filename extension for BeanShell is*****.bsh*. However, you can add a*****.java*file to the BeanShell scripting language.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249566996 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 06562: Using Common BPMN Elements

- page_id: `249566996`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249566996/Using+Common+BPMN+Elements
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-08-18T18:22:28.209+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > BPMN modeling > Using Cameo Business Modeler Plugin
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

148700809

148700810

148700810

Some BPMN elements can not be represented on any BPMN diagram. You can use these elements as data types for specifying values of other element properties. This kind of elements can be created in the Containment tree.

To create a BPMN element in the Containment tree

1. Right-click a package in the Containment tree.
2. From the shortcut menu, select New Element > BPMN Element , and choose a BPMN element that you want to create.

To open an element Specification window, do one of the following

- Right-click a selected symbol and from shortcut menu, select Specification .
- Double-click a symbol on the Diagram pane or in the Model Browser.
- Select a symbol on the Diagram pane and press Enter.
- Add a model element to an owning model element in its Specification window. The second Specification window opens on top of the first. Use the Back to or Forward to arrow buttons for switching between windows.

148700808

**Related element**

- Common BPMN Elements

**Related external resource**

- Specification window

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243967124 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 06565: Using diagram tabs

- page_id: `243967124`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243967124/Using+diagram+tabs
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:50:20.795+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Diagramming
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

568043094

568043096

568043095

To split diagrams in new horizontal or vertical group

1. Do one of the following:
  - Right-click the diagram tab. The shortcut menu opens.
  - Select the open diagram tab, then drag it to the diagram pane. The shortcut menu opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='diagram_tab_shortcut_on_dgr.png']
2. Select New Horizontal Group or New Vertical Group to split the diagram pane and have more than one diagram opened at the same time.

To split diagrams, more than one diagram should be open.

To turn the diagram window to floating

- From the diagram tab shortcut menu, select Floating .

To show the diagram owner on the diagram tab

1. Select a package and create a diagram in it.
2. Right-click anywhere on the diagram, and from the shortcut menu, select Diagram Properties .
3. Set the Show Owner property to true .

##### Showing diagrams in Full Screen

If you want to see your diagrams in full screen and work exclusively from the diagram, use the **Show Tabs in Full-Screen** functionality. In a full-screen mode, all necessary modeling commands are visible with an option to hide, and the Model Browser is in the auto-hide mode. You can also set the interface components to be displayed or hidden.

To turn on diagram full-screen mode, do one of the following

- Double-click the diagram tab.
- From the diagram shortcut menu, select Show Tabs in Full Screen .
- From the View menu, select Show Tabs in Full Screen .
- Press F11. You can change the **Show Tabs in Full Screen** shortcut key in the **Environment Options** dialog, **Keyboard** option group.

You can turn off the diagram full-screen mode in the same way as turning it on.

###### Managing Interface Components in Diagram Full Screen Mode

The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space=''] is in the auto-hide mode when the diagram full-screen mode is turned on. The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='']tabs are located on the left side of the window. To display the browser, move the mouse pointer over the desired browser window tab (for example, on the Containment tab), and the tab opens.

The main toolbar is hidden when the diagram full-screen mode is turned on. To display the main toolbar, set the **Hide Toolbars in the Full Screen Mode** property to false in the **Environment Options** dialog, General pane, and **Display** option group.

The diagram toolbar is displayed when the diagram full-screen mode is turned on. Right-click the diagram toolbar to manage it.

568043093

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagramming' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249565384 space=MT version=4 -->
## PAGE 06566: Using drag and drop

- page_id: `249565384`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249565384/Using+drag+and+drop
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-09-16T13:26:30.051+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > Procedures
- labels: ['drag', 'drop', 'select-drag-drop-rule', 'drag-and-drop', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Create relationships or assign property values by dragging and dropping one element on to another right in the diagram pane.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

There are a number of predefined rules to create relationships or assign property values automatically. You may also set up your own drag-and-drop specifications between any elements.

To select a rule

1. Do one of the following:
  - Drag a selected element onto another, hold it for a few seconds and then release it. A shortcut menu is displayed.
  - Right-click an element and drag it onto another (This action is valid only for Windows operating system users). A shortcut menu is displayed.
2. From the shortcut menu, select one of the rules you want to apply.

The following table lists the predefined drag-and-drop rules. These rules define the possible results when dragging the source element on the target element.

| Source Element | Target Element | Created Relationship | Assigned Property |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Activity | Capability | Maps To Capability |  |
| Actual Condition | Actual Effect |  | Enabling Condition |
| Actual Condition | Actual Resource |  | Actual Condition |
| Actual Condition | Measurement |  | Environmental Context |
| Actual Enduring Task | Actual Enterprise Phase |  | Statement Task |
| Actual Environment | Actual Resource |  | Actual Condition |
| Actual Environment | Location Holder |  | Required Environment |
| Actual Environment | Measurement |  | Environmental Context |
| Actual Location | Actual Resource |  | Actual Condition |
| Actual Location | Location Holder |  | Physical Location |
| Actual Location | Measurement |  | Environmental Context |
| Actual Measurement Set | Measurable Element |  | Actual Measurement Set |
| Actual Organization | Actual Project Milestone |  | Actual Resource |
| Actual Organization | Actual Project | Responsible For |  |
| Actual Organization | Actual Risk |  | Actual Risk Owner |
| Actual Organization | Actual Risk |  | Affected Actual Resource |
| Actual Organization | Actual Strategic Phase | Organization In Phase |  |
| Actual Organization | Standard |  | Ratified By |
| Actual Organizational Resource | Competence | Provides Competence |  |
| Actual Organizational Resource | Value Item | Owns Value |  |
| Actual Organizational Resource | Operational Activity | Owns Process |  |
| Actual Outcome | Enterprise Goal | Impacted By |  |
| Actual Outcome | Strategic Asset | Creates |  |
| Actual Person | Actual Post | Fills Post |  |
| Actual Person | Actual Project Milestone |  | Actual Resource |
| Actual Person | Actual Project | Responsible For |  |
| Actual Person | Actual Risk |  | Actual Risk Owner |
| Actual Person | Actual Risk |  | Affected Actual Resource |
| Actual Post | Actual Project Milestone |  | Actual Resource |
| Actual Post | Actual Project | Responsible For |  |
| Actual Post | Actual Risk |  | Actual Risk Owner |
| Actual Post | Actual Risk |  | Affected Actual Resource |
| Actual Project Milestone | Actual Project Milestone | Milestone Dependency |  |
| Actual Project Milestone | Actual Resource |  | Milestone |
| Actual Project Milestone | Versioned Element |  | Version Released At Milestone |
| Actual Project Milestone | Versioned Element |  | Version Withdrawn At Milestone |
| Actual Project | Actual Project Milestone |  | Actual Resource |
| Actual Project | Actual Project | Project Sequence |  |
| Actual Project | Actual Risk |  | Affected Actual Resource |
| Actual Resource | Actual Project Milestone |  | Actual Resource |
| Actual Resource | Actual Risk |  | Affected Actual Resource |
| Actual Responsibility | Actual Project Milestone |  | Actual Resource |
| Actual Responsibility | Actual Risk |  | Affected Actual Resource |
| Actual State [Element] | Actual State | Sequence |  |
| Actual Strategic Phase | Capability | Phases |  |
| Actual Strategic Phase | Challenge | Impacted By |  |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>Create relationships or assign property values by dragging and dropping one element on to another right in the diagram pane.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Dragging and dropping Actual Organization onto Actual Project" ac:alt="Dragging and dropping Actual Organization onto Actual Project"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dragging_actual_organization_onto_actual_project.png" /></ac:image></p><p>There are a number of predefined rules to create relationships or assign property values automatically. You may also set up your own drag-and-drop specifications between any elements.</p><p>To select a rule</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<br /><ul><li>Drag a selected element onto another, hold it for a few seconds and then release it. A shortcut menu is displayed.</li><li>Right-click an element and drag it onto another (This action is valid only for Windows operating system users). A shortcut menu is displayed.</li></ul></li><li>From the shortcut menu, select one of the rules you want to apply.</li></ol><p>The following table lists the predefined drag-and-drop rules. These rules define the possible results when dragging the source element on the target element.</p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 53.5381%;"><colgroup><col style="width: <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243973850 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 06567: Using Eclipse to develop Groovy scripts

- page_id: `243973850`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243973850/Using+Eclipse+to+develop+Groovy+scripts
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T19:18:22.463+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Macro engine > Supported scripting languages
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Use the following three simple steps to develop and run a Groovy script in NetBeans:

1. Install Groovy-Eclipse Plugin.
2. Develop a Groovy script.

To install Groovy-Eclipse Plugin

1. Go to [https://github.com/groovy/groovy-eclipse](https://github.com/groovy/groovy-eclipse) .
2. Follow the instructions for installing Groovy-Eclipse Plugin.

To develop a Groovy Script

1. Create a Groovy project.
2. Right-click the project in Package Explorer and click Properties .
3. Click Java Build Path in the tree on the left-hand side. Click the Libraries tab on the right pane.
4. Click Add External JARs to add all the JAR files in <modeling tool name>/lib and in <modeling tool name>/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton/engines/groovy-2.0.1/embeddable/groovy-all-2.0.1.jar .
5. Click OK to close the dialog.
6. Create a new Groovy class in the project. Remove a class declaration, and then put the script there.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>Use the following three simple steps to develop and run a Groovy script in NetBeans:</p><ol><li>Install Groovy-Eclipse Plugin.</li><li>Develop a Groovy script.</li></ol><p>To install Groovy-Eclipse Plugin</p><hr /><ol><li>Go to <em><a href="https://github.com/groovy/groovy-eclipse">https://github.com/groovy/groovy-eclipse</a></em>.</li><li>Follow the instructions for installing Groovy-Eclipse Plugin.</li></ol><p>To develop a Groovy Script</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a Groovy project.</li><li>Right-click the project in Package Explorer and click <strong>Properties</strong>.</li><li>Click Java Build Path in the tree on the left-hand side. Click the <strong>Libraries</strong> tab on the right pane.</li><li>Click <strong>Add External JARs</strong> to add all the JAR files in <em>&lt;modeling tool name&gt;/lib</em> and in <em>&lt;modeling tool name&gt;/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton/engines/groovy-2.0.1/embeddable/groovy-all-2.0.1.jar</em>.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> to close the dialog.</li><li>Create a new Groovy class in the project. Remove a class declaration, and then put the script there.</li></ol></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243969018 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 06568: Using Find operation

- page_id: `243969018`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243969018/Using+Find+operation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-07T17:23:51.993+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Model analysis > Specifying criteria for querying model
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Useful information

68780004

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Useful information

68780016

68780003

**On this page**

****

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Useful information

68780006

The modeling tool utilizes a Find operation that allows you to automatically search for model elements, represent relations between these elements, or gather their [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom traceability rules' space=''] values each time you update your model. The Find operation is customizable, so you can change the default values of all the properties for the selected operation at any any time. The Find operation can be accessed and used:

- In a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Relation Map' space=''] to represent relations between elements.
- In a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency Matrix' space=''] to associate elements in rows and columns of a matrix.
- From the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space=''] of a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Smart Package' space='MED'] to query a model and gather specific elements.
- From the Specification window of the selected element to calculate/gather the values of derived properties.
- In a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generic table' space=''] to calculate/gather the values of derived properties.
- From the Specification window of an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating executable opaque behaviors' space=''] to specify the body of a StructuredExpression.

#### TIP: Tip

Tip

If you would like to simply find the desired model element, and don't need to automatically query your model every time you update it, use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Find dialog' space='']**Find** dialog instead.

To use a Find operation, you need to specify it as criteria. The next section describes how to complete this task.

##### Specifying a Find operation as criteria

Specifying a Find operation as criteria allows you to automatically query your model in order to find and render specific model elements, relations between these elements, or gather their derived properties depending on where the operation is used (as mentioned above).

To specify a Find operation as criteria

1. Open the dialog for [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Getting started with specifying criteria' space=''] .
2. Select Find on the left side of the open dialog. [ATTACHMENT filename='Find_criterion.png']
3. Change the **Operation Name**, if needed.
4. Select the **Style** to represent the specified Find criteria. Note that the style selection is available only in the **Relation Criteria** and **Dependency Criteria** dialogs.
5. Specify the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Find dialog' space=''] , and click OK .

#### TIP: Useful information

Useful information

Selecting the**Search in all texts** check box allows you to search for text in Documentation and TODO properties.

##### expertAdvanced Manipulations

###### Specifying the Find operation properties

To specify the Find operation properties

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Getting started with specifying criteria' space=''] .
2. On the left side of the dialog, expand the Find operation.
3. Select the property you want to specify, and, in the property specification panel on the right side of the dialog, specify the value of the selected property. [ATTACHMENT filename='find_relation_criteria_dialog.png']
4. Click OK . The selected Find operation is now customized to query a model according to your specified properties. You can use a specific Find operation as an argument of another operation. In other words, you can create a new Find, Filter, Implied Relation, Exclude, Script, Union or Collection operation, and use the results of a specific Find operation as its property.

###### **find as property**To use the expression of a Find operation as a property of another operation

To use the expression of a Find operation as a property of another operation

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Getting started with specifying criteria' space=''] .
2. Select Find on the left side of the dialog.
3. In the criteria specification panel on the right side of the dialog, click the **Use as** button, and select the desired command that corresponds to the operation you want to create as shown below. A new operation is created. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Specify other parameters of the created operation, and click OK . You now have a new operation that has been created with the selected Find operation as one of its properties.

68794211

**Related pages**

- Specifying criteria for querying model
  - Getting started with specifying criteria
  - Using Simple Navigation
  - Using Metachain Navigation
  - Using Implied Relations
  - Creating new operations
    - Calling operations from the model
    - Creating script operations
