You can solve this problem by doing one of the following:</p><ul><li>Deploy Web Publisher 2.0 report on web server, or</li><li>Start Google Chrome with &quot;<strong>--allow-file-access-from-files</strong>&quot; argument by following the steps below:<ol><li>Close the web browser (Chrome) instances.</li><li>Open the web browser with &quot;<strong>--allow-file-access-from-files</strong>&quot; argument.<br /><br />2.1 Right click on web browser launcher and select <strong>Properties</strong>.</li></ol></li></ul><p style="margin-left: 120.0px;"><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="web2.0PropertiesSetting.png" /></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 120.0px;"><br /></p><p style="margin-left: 120.0px;">2.2 Add this argument &quot;<strong>--allow-file-access-from-files</strong>&quot; next to the web browser installation path.</p><h6 style="margin-left: 120.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="GoogleSettings.png" /></ac:image></h6><p>2.3 Open the web browser instance from the launcher.</p><p>2.4 Open the generated Web 2.0 report.</p><p>2.5 Start the web browser from the launcher or command line that contains &quot;<strong>--allow-file-access-from-files</strong>&quot; argument before.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6d397470-695b-400e-a7b2-c25dd09c32ba"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Web Publisher 2.0 report is also supported by Firefox.  You can configure Firefox before opening Web Publisher 2.0 report by doing the following:</p><ol><li>In the Firefox address box, type <strong>about:config</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="config.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Find the <strong>security.fileuri.strict_origin_policy</strong> parameter and set its parameter value to <strong>false</strong>.</li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="afebbfc6-1ea8-4b03-8601-7c75a7c65a56"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can also open Web Publisher 2.0 on Safari by doing the following:</p><ol><li>Open Safari.</li><li>On the toolbar of the browser, click <strong>Safari</strong> and select <strong>Preferences</strong>. The Preference dialog opens.</li><li>Click the <strong>Advanced</strong> tab and select the <strong>Show Develop menu in menu bar</strong> option. Close the dialog.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Safari Preference Advanced Dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>On the toolbar of the browser, click <strong>Develop</strong> and select <strong>Disable Local File Restrictions</strong>.</li></ol><p>Safari is now able to open Web Publisher 2.0.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1f21522f-f183-4810-80ad-7459588add25"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>In Report Wizard 2021x Refresh 2 and later, <strong>$exporter2 (DOM : Exporter2)</strong> is no longer used, and <strong>$exporter3(XMLStream : ExporterTool3)</strong> is used by default instead.</p><p>If you want to continue using Exporter2, use the following line in <em>index.html</em> of the Web Publisher 2.0 template:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bb83dadc-d0df-4ebf-b07e-ba106357622e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[XMLRequest.send('$exporter2.exportAll(true, $linkedFile)', renderBrowser);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Cameo Collaborator is developed for replacing Web Publisher Collaboration report. This is a web-based solution for publishing and reviewing models online. See more detail at <a href="https://www.3ds.com/products-services/catia/products/no-magic/cameo-collaborator-for-teamwork-cloud/">Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud Documentation</a></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d8fa182a-8527-4cf7-9954-6dc11f237cea"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">347568348</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="72500a1f-e65d-4d46-90c8-87b0347b80fd" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243971308 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 06618: What is a Template?

- page_id: `243971308`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243971308/What+is+a+Template
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:52:10.889+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Introduction
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A template, in Velocity, is a text file that tells Velocity what the output should look like. It contains a document page style, layout, header, footer, and static text just like any other template that comes with most of the Word Processor programs. However, a Velocity template also contains specific placeholders for Java objects and Velocity Template Language scripts which will tell the Velocity Engine what and how to print the information in the output.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A template, in Velocity, is a text file that tells Velocity what the output should look like. It contains a document page style, layout, header, footer, and static text just like any other template that comes with most of the Word Processor programs. However, a Velocity template also contains specific placeholders for Java objects and Velocity Template Language scripts which will tell the Velocity Engine what and how to print the information in the output.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249561899 space=MT version=4 -->
## PAGE 06619: Whitebox ICD Table

- page_id: `249561899`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249561899/Whitebox+ICD+Table
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-09-08T16:49:22.273+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > SysML v1 diagram descriptions
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The purpose of Whitebox ICD Table is to show how [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Part Property' space='MED'] are connected via ports/interfaces. This table collects all parts of the context element. Those parts are connected through the ports/interfaces using [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MED']. You can visually identify what kind of ports/interfaces (Port A and Port B) and flows (Item Flow) are used to connects two Part Properties (Part A and Part B).

In the row 1 of the following figure, the*Controller* Part Property sends the *control* Signal to the *Boiler* Part Property through the ports *b* and *c*. The *Controller* receives the *Status* signal from the *Boiler* through the Ports *c* and *b*.In the row 2, the Part *Controller* sends the flow *Elec Power* to the Part *Boiler* through the Ports *bp* and *p in*. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Interface Control Document tables' space='']>]]>

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']An example of Whitebox ICD Table.

In the created table, a row represents the Connector and the columns represent features of that Connector. You can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with columns' space=''] them ifno longer need.

Using **Show Parts**, you can easily filter connectors and choose which ones should be displayed in the table or hidden from it. In the **Show Parts** filter, you can:

- See all the connectors used in a defined context.
- Easier identify and multi-select the needed connectors (they are grouped by parts).
- Scroll through the list with the possibility to expand or collapse groups.
- Use the search field which allows to quickly find needed connectors.

To choose which connectors should be displayed or hidden

1. In the Whitebox ICD Table toolbar, click **Show Parts**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. In the **Show Parts** dialog, select the connectors you want to hide or display.
3. Click **OK**.

- If the part is in a partially selected or empty state, clicking it selects all the connectors under that part. If the part is in fully selected state, clicking it cancels all the connector selections.
- The connectors are represented under both end parts, so if you choose the connector under one end, the second end will be selected as well. It works the same way when clearing the selection.

A Whitebox ICD Table consists of the following columns.

| Column name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| # | A row number. |
| Part A | Displays a Part that is connected with Part B. |
| Port A | Displays a Port that is connected with Port B. |
| Port A Features | Displays all the properties of the Port A. |
| Item Flow | Displays the flow kind and its direction which flows through the Connector between two Parts (Part A and Part B). |
| Port B | Displays a Port that is connected with Port A. |
| Port B Features | Displays all the properties of the Port B. |
| Part B | Displays a Part that is connected with Part A. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The <em>Controller</em> receives the <em>Status</em> signal from the <em>Boiler</em> through the Ports <em>c</em> and <em>b</em>.</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> In the row 2,  the Part <em>Controller</em> sends the flow <em>Elec Power</em> to the Part <em>Boiler</em> through the Ports <em>bp</em> and <em>p in</em>.</span> <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating Interface Control Document tables" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn how to create and work in the Interface Control Documents >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Whitebox ICD Table" ac:alt="Whitebox ICD Table"><ri:attachment ri:filename="whitebox_icd_table.png" /></ac:image>An example of Whitebox ICD Table.</span></h6><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">In the created table, a row represents the Connector and the columns represent <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">features of that Connector. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249567516 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 06620: WhiteSpace

- page_id: `249567516`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249567516/WhiteSpace
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T18:31:02.270+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > Data-related modeling > XML schemas > XML schema mapping to UML elements
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Maps to a UML Attribute with the stereotype XSDwhiteSpace. The name and type of such an attribute do not make sense.

- value – to Attribute initial value.

#### PANEL: whiteSpace XML representation summary

whiteSpace XML representation summary

```text
<whiteSpace
```

```text
fixed = boolean : false
```

```text
id = ID
```

```text
value = (collapse | preserve | replace)
```

```text
{any attributes with non-schema namespace…}>
```

```text
Content: (annotation?)
```

```text
</whiteSpace>
```

#### PANEL: whiteSpace XML code sample

whiteSpace XML code sample

```text
The following example is the datatype definition for the token built-in derived datatype.
```

```text
<simpleType name='token'>
```

```text
<restriction base='normalizedString'>
```

```text
<whiteSpace value='collapse'/>
```

```text
</restriction>
```

```text
</simpleType>
```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Maps to a UML Attribute with the stereotype XSDwhiteSpace. The name and type of such an attribute do not make sense.</p><ul><li style="text-align: left;">value – to Attribute initial value.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="744d5a95-b52a-4dd5-9f9f-f43876ef51ff"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">whiteSpace XML representation summary</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><pre>&lt;whiteSpace</pre><pre>	fixed = boolean : false</pre><pre>	id = ID</pre><pre>	value = (collapse | preserve | replace)</pre><pre>	{any attributes with non-schema namespace…}&gt;</pre><pre>	Content: (annotation?)</pre><pre>&lt;/whiteSpace&gt;</pre></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c4463701-752f-4a2f-88f6-a7ab5ac60fbf"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">whiteSpace XML code sample</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><pre>The following example is the datatype definition for the token built-in derived datatype.</pre><pre>&lt;simpleType name='token'&gt;</pre><pre>	&lt;restriction base='normalizedString'&gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;whiteSpace value='collapse'/&gt;</pre><pre>	&lt;/restriction&gt;</pre><pre>&lt;/simpleType&gt;</pre></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243966639 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 06621: Why is prototyping so important?

- page_id: `243966639`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243966639/Why+is+prototyping+so+important
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:50:07.311+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Diagrams > Extension diagrams > User Interface Modeling diagram
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Various versions of User Interfaces are tested to see how clients respond. Client response is important for main dialogs, such as a sign-up screen for a website or an e-commerce application. Working with User Interface prototypes instead of "real" User Interfaces enables you to work with small details (such as color or the position of a button), as well as a substantially reduced designer and programmer overhead in the design phase. By building mock-ups or prototypes, you receive fast feedback from prospective users for future designs. User Interface prototyping makes this possible. The feedback loop makes for quicker sophisticated designs that work for everybody, which is what matters.

You can build mock-ups or prototypes to meet the following objectives:

- Integrate GUI development with UML specifications. Since this is a new field with increased design capability, it is easier to see the missing parts in UML or User Interface modeling.
- Enable business analysts to quickly gather requirements via permanent feedback from prospective users, making it easier to obtain all the information needed.
- Create browsable User Interface prototypes/ GUI simulation/ Story-boarding. Several prototypes can be connected via hyperlinks with one another and then be presented together in a report to simulate the workflow of an application.
- Resolve flow issues. It is easier to think through a problem when a User-related Interface can be changed quickly.
- Get "buy-in" from stakeholders. A visual representation of a particular User Interface can be shown more rapidly.
- Run a usability test before full production so that potential errors in usability, like overfilled screens or a usage too complicated, can be avoided.
- Test a series of interaction widgets. Consider, for example, that another text field or button would be good on a screen. Since modifying a prototype is easy, it is much simpler to present these suggestions to others.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Various versions of User Interfaces are tested to see how clients respond. Client response is important for main dialogs, such as a sign-up screen for a website or an e-commerce application. Working with User Interface prototypes instead of &quot;real&quot; User Interfaces enables you to work with small details (such as color or the position of a button), as well as a substantially reduced designer and programmer overhead in the design phase. By building mock-ups or prototypes, you receive fast feedback from prospective users for future designs. User Interface prototyping makes this possible. The feedback loop makes for quicker sophisticated designs that work for everybody, which is what matters.</p><p>You can build mock-ups or prototypes to meet the following objectives:</p><ul><li>Integrate GUI development with UML specifications. Since this is a new field with increased design capability, it is easier to see the missing parts in UML or User Interface modeling.</li><li>Enable business analysts to quickly gather requirements via permanent feedback from prospective users, making it easier to obtain all the information needed.</li><li>Create browsable User Interface prototypes/ GUI simulation/ Story-boarding. Several prototypes can be connected via hyperlinks with one another and then be presented together in a report to simulate the workflow of an application.</li><li>Resolve flow issues. It is easier to think through a problem when a User-related Interface can be changed quickly.</li><li>Get &quot;buy-in&quot; from stakeholders. A visual representation of a particular User Interface can be shown more rapidly.</li><li>Run a usability test before full production so that potential errors in usability, like overfilled screens or a usage too complicated, can be avoided.</li><li>Test a series of interaction widgets. Consider, for example, that another text field or button would be good on a screen. Since modifying a prototype is easy, it is much simpler to present these suggestions to others.</li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249563814 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 06622: Working with A8 Standards Taxonomy

- page_id: `249563814`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249563814/Working+with+A8+Standards+Taxonomy
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T16:59:24.715+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > NAF 4.0 > Architecture Meta-Data viewpoint > A8 Standards
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Creating a diagram

To create a Standards Taxonomy diagram

1. In the Containment Tree, select the A8 and do one of the following:
  - From the selected package's shortcut menu, select Create Diagram > A8 Standards Taxonomy . [ATTACHMENT filename='A8_diagram.png']
  - In the modeling tool's main menu, click Create Diagram , search for A8 Standards Taxonomy, and select it.
2. Name a diagram or leave it with the default name.

##### Creating an element

When the diagram is created, you can start creating the appropriate elements. An example is described using the Standard element, but the same is valid for other elements.

To create an element in a diagram

1. In the diagram palette, click the Standard (or another appropriate element) and then click the appropriate place on the diagram pane. [ATTACHMENT filename='A8_create_standard.png']
2. Name the element.

To create an element in the Containment tree

1. Right-click the A8 package, and from the shortcut menu, select Create Element . Search for the Standard (or another appropriate element) and select it. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Name the element.
3. Drag the created element from the Containment tree to the diagram pane.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To create the elements from other resources (e.g. Word, Excel, HTML)

1. Copy a list in your resource.
2. In a diagram, press Ctrl+V and from the Paste Special dialog, choose Element .
3. From the Select Type dialog, choose Standard (or another appropriate element).

##### Connect the elements

When you have Standard (or another appropriate element) created, you can start connecting them using the Generalization relationship.

To connect the elements with the Generalization relationship

1. Select an element from which the Generalization will be drawn.
2. From the diagram palette, choose Generalization.
3. Connect the Standard elements.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

If needed, you can make the Generalization target element abstract.

To make an element abstract

1. Select an element and open its [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space=''] .
2. Make sure that All is chosen for Properties .
3. Search for Is Abstract and set it to true .

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To view the inherited properties

1. Select an element and open its [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space=''] .
2. Click Attributes, Properties, or Relations property group.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] [IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h3>Creating a diagram</h3><p><br /></p><p>To create a Standards Taxonomy diagram</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Containment Tree, select the A8 and do one of the following:<ul><li> From the selected package's shortcut menu, select <strong>Create Diagram </strong>&gt;<strong> A8 Standards Taxonomy</strong>.<br /><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:height="367" ac:width="302"><ri:attachment ri:filename="A8_diagram.png" /></ac:image></li><li>In the modeling tool's main menu, click <strong>Create Diagram</strong>, search for <strong>A8 Standards Taxonomy, </strong>and select it.</li></ul></li><li>Name a diagram or leave it with the default name.</li></ol><h3>Creating an element</h3><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">When the diagram is created, you can start creating the appropriate elements</span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">. An example is described using the Standard element, but the same is valid for other elements.</span></p><p><br /></p><p>To create an element in a diagram</p><hr /><ol><li>In the diagram palette, click the <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Standard </span>(or another appropriate element) and then click the appropriate place on the diagram pane.<br /><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:height="243" ac:width="445"><ri:attachment ri:filename="A8_create_standard.png" /></ac:image></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Name the element.<br /></span></li></ol><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">To create an element in the Containment tree</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Right-click the A8 package, and from the shortcut menu, select </span><strong>Create Element</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">. Search for the Standard </span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">(or another appropriate element)</span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> and select it.<br /><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:height="189" ac:width="279"><ri:attachment ri:filename="A8_create_standard_containment_tree.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></li><li>Name the element.</li><li>Drag the created element from the Containment tree to the diagram pane.<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></li></ol><p><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:height="176" ac:width="812"><ri:attachment ri:filename="A8_drag_standard.png" /></ac:image></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></p><p>To create the elements from other resources (e.g. Word, Excel, HTML)</p><hr /><ol><li>Copy a list in your resource.</li><li>In a diagram, press Ctrl+V and from the <strong>Paste Special</strong> dialog, choose <strong>Element</strong>. </li><li>From the <strong>Select Type</strong> dialog, choose <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Standard</span> (or another appropriate element).</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e95dfc12-8507-4b0f-a54d-55c89892323c"><ac:rich-text-body>For more information about creating the elements from other resources, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating elements from other resources" /></ac:link>.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Connect the elements</h3><p><br /></p><p>When you have <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Standard</span> (or another appropriate element) created, you can start connecting them using the Generalization relationship.</p><p><br /></p><p>To connect the elements with the Generalization relationship</p><hr /><ol><li>Select an element from which the Generalization will be drawn.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">From the diagram palette, choose Generalization.</p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Connect the Standard elements.</span></p></li></ol><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><ac:image ac:border="true"><ri:attachment ri:filename="A8_standards_connect_generalization.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></p><p>If needed, you can make the Generalization target element abstract.</p><p><br /></p><p>To make an element abstract</p><hr /><ol><li>Select an element and open its <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>.</li><li>Make sure that <strong>All</strong> is chosen for <strong>Properties</strong>.</li><li>Search for <strong>Is Abstract</strong> and set it to <em>true</em>.</li></ol><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="standards_is_abstract.png" /></ac:image></p><p><br /></p><p>To view the inherited properties</p><hr /><ol><li>Select an element and open its <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>.</li><li>Click Attributes, Properties, or Relations property group.</li></ol><p><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="233"><ri:attachment ri:filename="inherited_measurements.png" /></ac:image>      <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="standards_inherited.png" /></ac:image></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249563824 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 06623: Working with A8 Standards Taxonomy Table

- page_id: `249563824`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249563824/Working+with+A8+Standards+Taxonomy+Table
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T13:16:05.640+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > NAF 4.0 > Architecture Meta-Data viewpoint > A8 Standards
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The**Standards Taxonomy Table (A8)**provides all the available technical and non-technical standards, guidances, and policies.

To add a new Protocol, Protocol Stack, or Standard in the Standards Taxonomy table

1. In the table toolbar, click the Add New button and choose Protocol, Protocol Stack, or Standard. A new row is added.
2. Double-click the cells to enter a Name, Definition, and URI for the Protocol, Protocol Stack, or Standard.

To add an existing standard in the Standards Taxonomy table

1. In the diagram toolbar, click the Add Existing button.
2. In the Select Standard dialog, choose the desired Protocol, Protocol Stack, or Standard.
3. Double-click the cells to enter a Name, Definition, and URI for the Protocol, Protocol Stack, or Standard.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The<span> </span><strong>Standards Taxonomy Table (A8)</strong><span> </span>provides all the available technical and non-technical standards, guidances, and policies.</p><p><br /></p><p>To add a new Protocol, Protocol Stack, or Standard in the Standards Taxonomy table</p><hr /><ol><li>In the table toolbar, click the<span> </span><strong>Add New</strong><span> </span>button and choose Protocol, Protocol Stack, or Standard. A new row is added.</li><li>Double-click the cells to enter a Name, Definition, and URI for the Protocol, Protocol Stack, or Standard.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To add an existing standard in the Standards Taxonomy table</p><hr /><ol><li>In the diagram toolbar, click the <strong>Add Existing</strong><span> </span>button.</li><li>In the <strong>Select Standard </strong>dialog, choose the desired Protocol, Protocol Stack, or Standard.</li><li>Double-click the cells to enter a Name, Definition, and URI for the Protocol, Protocol Stack, or Standard.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243966431 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 06624: Working with Activity diagram

- page_id: `243966431`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243966431/Working+with+Activity+diagram
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-08T17:18:16.639+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Diagrams > UML diagrams > Activity diagram
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Behavior Creation Mode

You can use the **Behavior Creation Mode** to automatically create an Activity after creating a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Call Behavior Action' space='MED'] in the . The newly created Activity will be set as the behavior of the Call Behavior Action.

To turn on the **Behavior Creation Mode** in an Activity diagram

- In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space=''], click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. The****Behavior Creation Mode works separately for each project.

When the **Behavior Creation Mode** is on:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Behavior' space='MED']A Behavior is created together with the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Call Behavior Action' space='MED']. It inherits the type of the Call Behavior Action owner.
- If a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Call Behavior Action' space='MED'] and its [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Behavior' space='MED'] are not titled, type a name on the Call Behavior Action shape on the diagram, and the typed name will be set as the Behavior's name automatically.

##### Creating Actions by pasting the copied list

You can copy the item list from the text editor (Word, Notepad, etc.), web page, PDF file, PowerPoint slide and paste it in the Activity diagram to create actions.

To create Actions by pasting items from the list

1. Copy the list.
2. Go to the diagram and press Ctrl+V.
3. Select an Action.
4. In the question dialog click Yes if you need to connect the Actions with Control Flow relationship.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Smart layout

###### Dynamiccenterlines

The centerlines are displayed only when the center of the shape that was moved or newly drawn is located near the center of a preexisting shape in the diagram. These lines help you to easily align shapes when drawing a diagram.

When the center of the shape that was moved coincides with a center of any shape that is placed to its right or left, a horizontal centerline appears. When the center of the shape is close to any center of a shape that is located above or below it, a vertical centerline appears.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To switch off the dynamic centerlines

- Click the Show Centerlines button [IMAGE alt='' src=''] in the diagram toolbar, or press C .
- From the Options main menu, select Environment . In the Environment Options dialog, click the Diagram category, and clear the Show Centerlines in Flow Diagrams check box in the Display properties group.

###### Diagram orientation

The diagram orientation is used to assign the correct rectilinear path breaks and draw paths between the Activity diagram shapes. The paths can be drawn from side to side, or from the lower to the upper shape borders.

#### TIP: Example

Example

If you have a vertical diagram orientation and shapes are not in the same centerline, the paths will be connected from the lower border of the first shape to the upper border of the next shape, adding break points: 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
If you have a horizontal diagram orientation, the paths will be connected from the side border of the first shape to the next side border of the second shape, adding breakpoints: 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To change the diagram orientation

- From the Options main menu, select Project . In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Setting project options' space='']**Project Options** dialog , expand Symbol Styles > Default > Diagram group (if it is not expanded by default) and, in the options list, change the value for the Diagram Orientation property.
- From the diagram pane shortcut menu, select Diagram Properties, or press Shift+Enter to open the Diagram Properties dialog. Change the value for the Diagram Orientation property in that dialog.

##### Deleting from Activity diagrams

- Note that after deleting the last symbol of an element from the Activity diagram, the element will be automatically removed from the model. This does not affect [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Pin' space='MED'] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity Parameter Node' space='MED'] .
- If a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Call Behavior Action' space='MED'] that has an assigned [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Behavior' space='MED'] is deleted from the Activity diagram, then this Behavior is also deleted from the model.

This is valid if the assigned Behavior is editable and does not have more usages, i.e. there is not a Call Behavior Action that has a reference to this Behavior.

##### Displaying inner elements

To display inner elements

- When creating a new Activity diagram, right-click an Activity in the Containment tree, click Create Diagram , and then click Activity Diagram .

Inner elements are displayed on the Activity diagram pane automatically upon creating the first diagram under the Activity element.

- When modifying a previously created Activity diagram, select the diagram pane and do one of the following:

- From the shortcut menu, click**Display**>**Display Inner Elements**. 
- On the diagram toolbar click[IMAGE alt='' src=''],and select**Display Inner Elements**.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dragging objects' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating elements from other resources' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Behavior Creation Mode</h3><p>You can use the <strong>Behavior Creation Mode</strong> to automatically create an Activity after creating a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Call Behavior Action" /></ac:link> in the <ac:link><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Activity Diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. The newly created Activity will be set as the behavior of the Call Behavior Action.</p><p>To turn on the <strong>Behavior Creation Mode</strong> in an Activity diagram</p><hr /><ul><li data-uuid="f7f688d3-8194-4555-a8de-2c2a36e65700"><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Understanding the user interface" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[diagram palette]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, click <span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><ac:image ac:title="Behavior Creation Mode" ac:alt="Behavior Creation Mode"><ri:attachment ri:filename="automatic_behavior_creation.png" /></ac:image></span>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8a792f8e-361c-4f23-8d0f-6accbb0243b6"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span class="confluence-link">The<strong> </strong>Behavior Creation Mode works separately for each project.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ul><p>When the <strong>Behavior Creation Mode</strong> is on:</p><ul><li data-uuid="5461f80e-69d1-4096-8254-2f33cfa96b32"><span><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Behavior" /><ac:link-body><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">A</span> Behavior</ac:link-body></ac:link> is created together with the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Call Behavior Action" /></ac:link>. It inherits the type of the Call Behavior Action owner.</span></li><li data-uuid="5bff5018-b13f-4635-9e62-d05e822b69dd">If a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Call Behavior Action" /></ac:link> and its <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Behavior" /></ac:link> are not titled, type a name on the Call Behavior Action shape on the diagram, and the typed name will be set as the Behavior's name automatically.</li></ul><h3 class="with-breadcrumbs">Creating Actions by pasting the copied list</h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">You can copy the item list from the text editor (Word, Notepad, etc.), web page, PDF file, PowerPoint slide and paste it in the Activity diagram to create actions.</span></p><p><br /></p><p>To create Actions by pasting items from the list</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="3fddb0fd-21e6-455f-80ee-656cae659749">Copy the list.</li><li data-uuid="0a9704e2-faf8-408b-b7da-404359e31b02">Go to the diagram and press Ctrl+V.</li><li data-uuid="d18fbfc3-e905-4456-b73e-61e03cdca3ab">Select an Action.</li><li data-uuid="606dbba7-f2e8-416a-b2cc-da92a108a6a3">In the question dialog click <strong>Yes</strong> if you need to connect the Actions with Control Flow relationship.</li></ol><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="paste_list_to_create_activities.png" /></ac:image></p><h3 class="with-breadcrumbs">Smart layout</h3><h4><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Dynamic</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span>centerlines</h4><p>The centerlines are displayed only when the center of the shape that was moved or newly drawn is located near the center of a preexisting shape in the diagram. These lines help you to easily align shapes when drawing a diagram.</p><p>When the center of the shape that was moved coincides with a center of any shape that is placed to its right or left, a horizontal centerline appears. When the center of the shape is close to any center of a shape that is located above or below it, a vertical centerline appears.</p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><ac:image ac:alt="Dynamic vertical centerline"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dynamic_centerline_vertical.gif" /></ac:image></span></p><p>To switch off the dynamic centerlines</p><hr /><ul><li data-uuid="95dc184d-3845-477f-8916-1d8709ba10c7">Click the <strong>Show Centerlines</strong> button <span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="show_centerlines.gif" /></ac:image></span> in the diagram toolbar, or press <strong>C</strong>.</li><li data-uuid="ab037ca1-79c7-466d-93c3-c0abb1601726">From the <strong>Options</strong> main menu, select <strong>Environment</strong>. In the<strong> Environment Options</strong> dialog, click the <strong>Diagram</strong> category, and clear the <strong>Show Centerlines in Flow Diagrams</strong> check box in the <strong>Display</strong> properties group.</li></ul><p><br /></p><h4>Diagram orientation</h4><p>The diagram orientation is used to assign the correct rectilinear path breaks and draw paths between the Activity diagram shapes. The paths can be drawn from side to side, or from the lower to the upper shape borders.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c7b66ba1-861d-466b-a5e1-a9b825bb1752"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p> If you have a vertical diagram orientation and shapes are not in the same centerline, the paths will be connected from the lower border of the first shape to the upper border of the next shape, adding break points: <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="diagram_orientation_vertical.gif" /></ac:image></span><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />If you have a horizontal diagram orientation, the paths will be connected from the side border of the first shape to the next side border of the second shape, adding breakpoints: <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="diagram_orientation_horizontal.gif" /></ac:image></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper">To change the diagram orientation</span></p><hr /><ul><li data-uuid="e45c0505-f209-437c-a5d8-9a8b990342cc">From the <strong>Options</strong> main menu, select <strong>Project</strong>. In the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Setting project options" /><ac:link-body><strong>Project Options</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>, expand <strong>Symbol Styles </strong>&gt;<strong> Default </strong>&gt;<strong> </strong><strong>Diagram</strong> group (if it is not expanded by default) and, in the options list, change the value for the <strong>Diagram Orientation</strong> property.</li><li data-uuid="bbd3d0af-2546-44ac-81cd-311de5c7b795">From the diagram pane shortcut menu, select <strong>Diagram Properties,</strong> or press Shift+Enter to open the <strong>Diagram Properties</strong> dialog. Change the value for the <strong>Diagram Orientation</strong> property in that dialog.</li></ul><h3 class="with-breadcrumbs">Deleting from Activity diagrams</h3><ul><li data-uuid="3c58492f-a9c8-41e8-82a9-a112dbd16986">Note that after deleting the last symbol of an element from the Activity diagram, the element will be automatically removed from the model. This does not affect <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Pin" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Pins]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Activity Parameter Node" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Activity Parameter Nodes]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li data-uuid="d7ef2b6e-b72e-4a6f-a1ef-484b3052da9c"><p>If a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Call Behavior Action" /></ac:link> that has an assigned <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Behavior" /></ac:link> is deleted from the Activity diagram, then this Behavior is also deleted from the model.</p></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d276b872-4811-4d21-98eb-dcfc6695d559"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>This is valid if the assigned Behavior is editable and does not have more usages, i.e. there is data-uuid="d9b4595f-4270-47d6-a1ad-59ae03348376">When creating a new Activity diagram, right-click an Activity in the Containment tree, click <strong>Create Diagram</strong>, and then click <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249564675 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 06625: Working with Actual Project Milestone Summary Table

- page_id: `249564675`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249564675/Working+with+Actual+Project+Milestone+Summary+Table
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T13:29:22.860+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > UAF 1.3 > Projects > Projects Roadmap
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**The** **Actual** **Project** **Milestone Summary** **Table (Pj-Rm)** depicts all actual project milestones in the model.

Adding the actual project milestones into the table, you can see their name, kind, start date, project, project status, version released, version withdrawn, and actual resource information. You will also be able to add or modify the actual project milestone information directly in this table.

To create a table

1. In the Model Browser, select the owner for the new table.
2. Do one of the following:
  - Press Ctrl+N.
  - On the main toolbar, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] .
  - From the main menu, select Diagrams > Create Diagram .
  - Open the Content diagram and on its palette, click the Create Diagram button.
3. In the opened window, search for Actual Project Milestone Summary Table . An empty table is created.

When you have a table, you can start filling it out. Here you can add new or already existing in the model elements.

To add new Actual Project Milestone

1. In the table, click the Add New button. The new Actual Projet Milestone is created.
2. Fill out the needed columns.

To add existing model elements

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the table toolbar, click **Add Existing**. In the open **Select Actual Project Milestone**dialog, choose which elements you need to add to the table. If you need to add more than one element, click the Multiple Selection button.
  - In the Containment tree, select Actual Project Milestone and drag it to the table.
2. Click **OK** when you are done.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><strong>The</strong> <strong>Actual</strong> <strong>Project</strong> <strong>Milestone Summary</strong> <strong>Table (Pj-Rm)</strong> depicts all actual project milestones in the model.</p><p>Adding the actual project milestones into the table, you can see their name, kind, start date, project, project status, version released, version withdrawn, and actual resource information. You will also be able to add or modify the actual project milestone information directly in this table.</p><p><br /></p><p>To create a table</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Model Browser, select the owner for the new table.</li><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>Press Ctrl+N.</li><li>On the main toolbar, click <span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><ac:image ac:alt="Create Diagram"><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_diagram.png" /></ac:image></span></span> .</li><li>From the main menu, select <strong>Diagrams</strong> &gt; <strong>Create Diagram</strong>.</li><li>Open the Content diagram and on its palette, click the <strong>Create Diagram</strong> button.</li></ul></li><li>In the opened window, search for <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="297c4c37-d037-447b-813d-084eb89828b1"> Actual Project Milestone Summary Table</ac:inline-comment-marker>.<br />An empty table is created.</li></ol><p>When you have a table, you can start filling it out. Here you can add new or already existing in the model elements.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="3cdcbc4d-8f66-463e-b4d7-372b6d650fc7">To add new Actual Project Milestone</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><hr /><ol><li>In the table, click the <strong>Add New</strong> button. The new Actual Projet Milestone is created.</li><li>Fill out the needed columns.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To add existing model elements</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the table toolbar, click <strong>Add Existing</strong>. In the open <strong>Select Actual Project Milestone </strong>dialog, choose which elements you need to add to the table.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="af0cc9ad-aae0-4c41-9090-ab112a1b858b"><ac:rich-text-body>If you need to add more than one element, click the Multiple Selection button.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>In the Containment tree, select Actual Project Milestone and drag it to the table.</li></ul></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Click <strong>OK</strong> when you are done.</p></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249564717 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 06626: Working with Actual Projects to Capabilities Mapping Matrix

- page_id: `249564717`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249564717/Working+with+Actual+Projects+to+Capabilities+Mapping+Matrix
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:07:16.830+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > UAF 1.3 > Projects > Projects Traceability
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1282638040

1282638053

1282638043

**Actual Projects to Capabilities Mapping Matrix** describes the mapping of programs and projects to capabilities to show how the specific projects and program elements help to achieve a capability.

The rows of this matrix are Actual Projects and the columns are Capabilities.

To create a Actual Projects to Capabilities Mapping Matrix

1. Specify Row Scope (Actual Projects).
2. Specify Column Scope (Capabilities).
3. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='refresh.png'] .

Actual Project maps to Capability through several transitive relationships which are displayed in the Matrix.

By default, the traceability is shown according to the following criteria:

- Mapping Using Milestone (Actual Resources and Resources) . The Actual Project owns Actual Project Milestones (Owned Milestones property) that are related to Actual Resources (milestone property) in which type is exhibiting (Exhibits relationship) Capabilities.
- Mapping Using Milestone (Resources Performers) . The Actual Project owns Actual Project Milestones (Owned Milestones property) that are related to Resources (version releasedAtMilestone and versionWithdrawn properties) which are exhibiting (Exhibits relationship) Capabilities.
- Mapping Using Milestone (Service Specification) . The Actual Project owns Actual Project Milestones (Owned Milestones property) that are related to Service Specifications (version releasedAtMilestone and versionWithdrawn properties) which are exhibiting (Exhibits relationship) Capabilities.
- Mapping Using Milestone (Actual Resources) . The Actual Project owns Actual Project Milestones (Owned Milestones property) that are related to Actual Resources which are exhibiting (Exhibits relationship) Capabilities.

You may hide some mapping rules from a Matrix by changing the Dependency criteria in the **Specification** window.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

1282638039

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with matrices' space='']

The Actual Project owns Actual Project Milestones (Owned Milestones property) that are related to Actual Resources (milestone property) in which type is exhibiting (Exhibits relationship) Capabilities.</li><li><strong>Mapping Using Milestone (Resources Performers)</strong>. The Actual Project owns Actual Project Milestones (Owned Milestones property) that are related to Resources (version releasedAtMilestone and versionWithdrawn properties) which are exhibiting (Exhibits relationship) Capabilities.</li><li><strong>Mapping Using Milestone (Service Specification)</strong>. The Actual Project owns Actual Project Milestones (Owned Milestones property) that are related to Service Specifications (version releasedAtMilestone and versionWithdrawn properties) which are exhibiting (Exhibits relationship) Capabilities.</li><li><strong>Mapping Using Milestone (Actual Resources)</strong>. The Actual Project owns Actual Project Milestones (Owned Milestones property) that are related to Actual Resources which are exhibiting (Exhibits relationship) Capabilities.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5b597bf3-c2a4-4b05-bcf3-8fb746561ed2"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You may hide some mapping rules from a Matrix by changing the Dependency criteria in the <strong>Specification</strong> window.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="mapping_criteria.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4534a74b-7d1d-459d-8539-0857b4ec5dec"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1282638039</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with matrices" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249564793 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 06627: Working with Actual Resource Taxonomy Matrix

- page_id: `249564793`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249564793/Working+with+Actual+Resource+Taxonomy+Matrix
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:07:21.171+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > UAF 1.3 > Actual Resources > Actual Resources Taxonomy
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1283014949

1283014961

1283014951

The **Actual Resource Taxonomy Matrix** (Ar-Tx) describes the mapping between the actual responsible resources and the actual responsibilities.

The Rows of this matrix are Actual Responsible Resources (Actual Organization, Actual Person, Actual Post) and the Columns are Actual Responsibility.

To create an Actual Resource Taxonomy Matrix

1. Specify the Rows Scope (Actual Responsible Resources);
2. Specify the Columns Scope (Actual Responsibility);
3. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='refresh.png'] .

Actual Responsible Resources map to Actual Responsibility through the Responsible For relationship.

To map the Actual Responsible Resource to Actual Responsibility

- Double-click the empty intersection between the desired elements.

To remove the relationship

- Double-click a full intersection between the desired elements.

1283014948

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249564782 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 06628: Working with Actual Resources Connectivity diagram

- page_id: `249564782`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249564782/Working+with+Actual+Resources+Connectivity+diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T13:29:25.505+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > UAF 1.3 > Actual Resources > Actual Resources Connectivity
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Actual Resources Connectivity diagram is used to model typical and actual forms of the organizational relationships.

To create an typical Actual Resources Connectivity diagram

1. Create Organizations, Posts, andPersons.
2. Display the generalizations (general-specific relationships) and compositions (whole-part relationships) between them.

To create an actual Actual Resources Connectivity diagram

1. Create Actual Organizations, Actual Posts, and Actual Persons.
2. Relate Actual Organizational Resources with Actual Organization Relationship. You can display:the Competenceof the Organizational Resources.the Operational Resource that is in charge of others using the Commands relationship.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Actual Resources Connectivity diagram is used to model typical and actual forms of the organizational relationships.</p><p>To create an typical Actual Resources Connectivity diagram</p><hr /><ol><li>Create <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Organizations, </span><span style="color: rgb(255,102,0);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Posts, <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">and </span><span style="color: rgb(255,102,0);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Persons</span></span>.</span></span></li><li>Display the generalizations (general-specific relationships) and compositions (whole-part relationships) between them.</li></ol><p> </p><p>To create an actual Actual Resources Connectivity diagram<span> </span></p><hr /><ol><li>Create Actual Organizations, <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Actual Posts, and <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Actual Persons</span>.</span></li><li><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Relate Actual Organizational Resources with <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Actual Organization Relationship</span></span>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="439ad953-f2b7-48c9-9049-4b629719adf6"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can display:</p><ul><li>the <span style="color: rgb(255,102,0);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Competence </span></span>of the Organizational Resources.</li><li>the Operational Resource that is in charge of others using the Commands relationship.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol>
````

To create an typical Actual Resources Structure diagram

1. Create Organizations, Posts, andPersons.

To create an actual Actual Resources Structure diagram

2. Relate Actual Organizational Resources with Actual Organization Relationship. You can display:the Competenceof the Organizational Resources.the Operational Resource that is in charge of others using the Commands relationship.

## PAGE 06630: Working with Actual Responsible Resources to Actual Projects Mapping Matrix

- page_id: `249564721`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249564721/Working+with+Actual+Responsible+Resources+to+Actual+Projects+Mapping+Matrix
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:07:17.297+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > UAF 1.3 > Projects > Projects Traceability
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1282670460

1282670463

1282670462

The Actual Responsible Resources to Actual Projects Mapping Matrix is an editable matrix displaying the responsibility of Actual Responsible Resources (Actual Organizations, Actual Persons, andActual Posts) for Projects.

The rows in the Actual Responsible Resources to Actual Projects Mapping Matrix represent Actual Responsible Resources (Actual Organizations, Actual Persons, and Actual Posts) and the columns represent Actual Projects.

To create an Actual Responsible Resources to Actual Projects Mapping Matrix

1. Specify the Row Scope (Actual Organizations, Actual Persons, and Actual Posts).
2. Specify the Column Scope (Actual Projects).
3. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='refresh.png'] .

Actual Responsible Resources map to Actual Projects using the Responsible For relationship.

##### Mapping an Actual Responsible Resource to an Actual Project

To map an Actual Responsible Resource to an Actual Project

1. Double-click an empty intersection.

##### Removing the relation from the matrix

To remove the relation

1. Double-click a full intersection between the desired elements.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249563929 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 06631: Working with Actual Strategic Phases Gantt Chart

- page_id: `249563929`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249563929/Working+with+Actual+Strategic+Phases+Gantt+Chart
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:06:29.005+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > UAF 1.3 > Strategy > Strategic Roadmap
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

The Actual Strategic Phases Gantt Chart shows how Actual Strategic Phases change over time. The rows represent the Actual Strategic Phase (Enterprise Mission,Actual Enduring Task,Whole Life Enterprise,Actual Enterprise Phase,Value Stream) in the roadmap. The bars represent the related information of the Actual Strategic Phase.

To increase the diagram readability, use legends. To learn how to use legends, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Legends' space=''] chapter.

##### Creating Actual Strategic Phases Gantt Chart

When you choose to create an Actual Strategic Phases Gantt Chart, **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagram Creation Wizard' space='']** opens.

To create an Actual Strategic Phases Gantt Chart from the wizard

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the main toolbar, click Create Diagram and select Actual Strategic Phases Gantt Chart .
  - Right-click the Strategic Roadmap package and select Create Diagram . Select Actual Strategic Phases Gantt Chart . [ATTACHMENT filename='create_actual_enterprise_phases_gantt_chart.png']
2. Follow the steps of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagram Creation Wizard' space=''].
3. Click Finish when you are done.

If you do not wish to use the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagram Creation Wizard' space='']** next time, clear the **Show the wizard next time, when I create Actual Strategic Phases Gantt Chart** check box.

To create an Actual Strategic Phases Gantt Chart manually when **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagram Creation Wizard' space='']** is disabled

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the main toolbar, click Create Diagram and select Actual Strategic Phases Gantt Chart .
  - Right-click the Strategic Roadmap package and select Create Diagram . Select Actual Strategic Phases Gantt Chart .
2. Type the chart name. (Optional)
3. Click Finish .

To restore the usage of the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagram Creation Wizard' space='']**

1. Select Options > Environment .
2. In the Environment Options dialog select the UAF options group.
3. In the General properties, set the Show Strategic Phasing Gantt Chart Creation Wizard property value to true .

##### Adding Actual Strategic Phase

To add an Actual Strategic Phase

1. On the Actual Strategic Phases Gantt Chart toolbar, click Add New and choose one of the following:
  - Add New Enterprise Mission
  - Add New Actual Enduring Task
  - Add New Whole Life Enterprise
  - Add New Actual Enterprise Phase
  - Add New Value Stream
2. Follow the steps of the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Actual Strategic Phase Assignment Wizard' space=''].**
3. Click Finish when you are done.

To add an existing Actual Strategic Phase

1. On the Actual Strategic Phases Gantt Chart toolbar, click Add Existing .
2. Follow the steps of the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Actual Strategic Phase Assignment Wizard' space=''].**
3. Click Finish when you are done.

##### Adding Sub Actual Strategic Phase

To add a new Sub Actual Strategic Phase

1. In the Gantt chart tree, select the Actual Strategic Phase for which you want to create a Sub Actual Strategic Phase.
2. In the shortcut menu of the selected Actual Strategic Phase, click Add New Sub Actual Strategic Phase .
3. Follow the steps of the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Actual Strategic Phase Assignment Wizard' space=''].**
4. Click Finish when you are done.

To add an existing Sub Actual Strategic Phase

1. In the Gantt chart tree, select the Actual Strategic Phase for which you want to add an existing Sub Actual Strategic Phase.
2. In the shortcut menu of the selected Actual Strategic Phase, click Add Existing Sub Actual Strategic Phase .
3. Follow the steps of the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Actual Strategic Phase Assignment Wizard' space=''].**
4. Click Finish when you are done.

When you choose to add new or existing Actual Strategic Phases, they will be listed for selection depending on the defined structure.

The element name in gray indicates that the element was used from another project and/or is read-only.

##### Displaying all suppressed or removed from the chart

When an Actual Strategic Phase is displayed in *italic*, it means that some sub elements were removed from the chart. You can restore them back with the click of one button.

To display all sub elements removed from the chart

1. Select the Actual Strategic Phase which sub elements you want to restore.
2. In the Actual Strategic Phases Gantt Chart toolbar, click the Display All button.

The **Display All** button also expands the suppressed structure.

##### Deleting elements and sub elements

You can either remove the element or sub element from the chart or delete it from the model.

To remove an element or sub elementfrom the chart

1. Select an element or sub element .
2. On the diagram toolbar, click the Remove From Chart button.

To delete an element or sub element from the model

1. Select an element or sub element .
2. On the diagram toolbar, click the Delete button.

##### Hiding or displaying columns

You can choose to hide or display the Actual Strategic Phase Name, Start Date, or End Date columns.

To show/hide the columns

1. In the diagram toolbar, click Show Columns.
2. In the menu, select the needed columns.

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Actual Strategic Phase Assignment Wizard' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing Gantt chart properties' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Filtering table data' space='']

The bars represent the related information of the Actual Strategic Phase.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="95a283d7-ac16-44af-bffb-bfda138b8c03"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To increase the diagram readability, use legends. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249563977 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 06632: Working with Actual Strategic Phases to Capabilities Mapping matrix

- page_id: `249563977`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249563977/Working+with+Actual+Strategic+Phases+to+Capabilities+Mapping+matrix
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:06:30.161+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > UAF 1.3 > Strategy > Strategic Traceability
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1283102180

1283102192

1283102182

**The Actual Strategic Phases to** **Capabilities** **Mapping Matrix (St-Tr)** describes the mapping between the required capabilities and the actual strategic phases that those actual strategic phases support directly or using the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Implied Relations' space=''].

The Rows of this matrix are Actual Strategic Phases and the Columns are Capabilities.

To create an Actual Strategic Phases to Capabilities Mapping Matrix

1. Specify Row Scope (Actual Strategic Phases);
2. Specify Column Scope (Capabilities);
3. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] .

Actual Strategic Phases map to Capabilities using Phases relationship.

To map an Actual Strategic Phase to a Capability

1. Double-click an empty intersection.

To remove the relation

1. Double-click a full intersection between the desired elements.

1283102179

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with matrices' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562920 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 06633: Working with AcV-2 diagram

- page_id: `249562920`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562920/Working+with+AcV-2+diagram
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:05:57.663+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > MODAF > MODAF Acquisition Viewpoint > MODAF. AcV-2 Programme Timelines
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

413746516

413746518

413746514

**On this page**

3

413746517

##### Creating AcV-2 diagram

When you choose to create AcV-2 diagram, AcV-2 Creation Wizard opens by default.

To create an AcV-2 diagram from the wizard

1. From the Diagrams menu, select Create Diagram .
2. Click AcV-2 Project Timeliness .
3. In the AcV-2 Project Timeliness dialog, click Add . AcV-2 Creation Wizard opens.
4. Specify the diagram name and create or select the owner for the diagram. Click Next .
5. Add the project(s). Click Next .
6. Set the chart properties.
7. Click **Finish**. If you do not wish to use the AcV-2 Creation Wizard next time, clear the check box near “Show the wizard next time, when I create AcV-2”.Also you can disable the wizard. Go to **Options** > **Environment**. In the **Environment Options** dialog go to **UAF** tab. In the **General** properties, set **Show PV-2 / AcV-2 / NPV-1 Creation Wizard each Time Creating New Chart** to False.

To create an AcV-2 diagram manually

1. From the Diagrams menu, select Create Diagram .
2. Click AcV-2 Project Timeliness .
3. In the AcV-2 Project Timeliness dialog click Add .
4. Specify the diagram name and create or select the owner of the diagram.
5. Click OK . The blank AcV-2 diagram is created.

You can select as a diagram owner a view or viewpoint that is already created in the UAF project template.

##### Adding actual projects to AcV-2 diagram

You can add a new or an existing actual project to the AcV-2 diagram.

To add a new actual project

1. On the AcV-2 diagram toolbar, click Add New > Actual Project . On the diagram pane, an empty line for the newly created actual project appears.
2. Specify a time frame for the actual project.

To add a new actual sub project

1. Select the actual project for which you want to create an actual sub project.
2. Do one of the following:
  - On the AcV-2 diagram toolbar, click Add New > Actual Sub Project .
  - On the shortcut menu of the selected actual project, click Add new Actual Sub Project .
3. Specify a time frame for the actual sub project.

To add an existing actual project

1. On the AcV-2 diagram toolbar, click Add Existing . The Select Element dialog opens.
2. In the dialog, select an Actual project (or several actual projects) and click the + button.
3. Click OK when you are done.

##### Deleting actual projects from AcV-2 diagram

To delete an actual project from the chart

1. Select an actual project.
2. On the diagram toolbar, click **Delete From Chart**. Actual Sub Projects can only be deleted from the model.

To delete an actual project from the model

1. Select an actual project. 
2. On the diagram toolbar, click **Delete**.

##### Relating actual projects in AcV-2 diagram

You can relate actual projects to a sequence using the following relation types:

- Finish to Start. Target actual project cannot start earlier than the source actual project finishes.
- Start to Start. The target actual project cannot start earlier than the source actual project starts.
- Finish to Finish. The target actual project cannot finish earlier than the source actual project finishes.
- Start to Finish. The target actual project cannot finish earlier than the source actual project starts.

You can change actual project dates by moving an actual project across the timeline. The Actual Project Sequence validation rule is executed while changing the related project dates. If dates in the project sequence violate the rule, the relation is highlighted in red.

To relate actual projects

1. Select two actual projects.
2. On the diagram toolbar, click Relate .
3. Select one of the available relation types:
  - Finish to Start
  - Start to Start
  - Finish to Finish
  - Start to Finish

To remove the relation between actual projects

1. Select two related actual projects.
2. On the diagram toolbar, click Relate .
3. Select None.

To resolve the incorrect actual project sequence

1. On the highlighted relation shortcut menu, click Validate .
2. Select one of the suggested solutions.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Available validation suggestions

If you change dates of an actual project containing actual sub projects, actual sub project dates change correspondingly.

##### Adding Milestones in AcV-2 diagram

To add a milestone

1. Select an actual project.
2. Do one of the following:
  - On the diagram toolbar, click Add New > Milestone .
  - On the selected actual project shortcut menu, click Add new Actual Project Milestone .
3. Select one of the available milestone kind.
4. The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Actual Project Milestone Creation Wizard' space=''] opens.
5. Specify milestone settings in the following wizard steps and click Finish when you are done.

##### Deleting milestones in AcV-2 diagram

To delete a milestone

1. Select a milestone.
2. From the milestone shortcut menu, select Delete .

##### Applying actual project status in AcV-2 diagram

To apply the actual project status to a new milestone

1. From the milestone shortcut menu, select Apply Status .
2. Specify the actual project status by following steps of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project Status Application Wizard' space=''] .

To edit the actual project status

1. From the milestone shortcut menu, select Edit Status .
2. Edit the actual project status by following the steps of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project Status Application Wizard' space=''] .

Specify Project Status

##### Removing actual project status in AcV-2 diagram

To remove the project status

1. From the milestone shortcut menu, select Edit Status .
2. In the opened [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project Status Application Wizard' space=''] , click the Remove project status indicator mark button.
3. Click Finish when you are done.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="68ad7570-5a2f-4d4b-8a61-f2c67fe1ddc8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">413746516</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e2f82754-09d3-4ac2-8732-ada794fee7e2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">413746518</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3fa04b1e-18d5-4a05-b14e-4b9baa4ef295"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">413746514</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3f3908bd-78b3-4476-bf81-736789e7b061"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="578ef716-1a5e-4322-8a22-d6c10d73bec7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">413746517</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><h3>Creating AcV-2 diagram</h3><p>When you choose to create AcV-2 diagram, AcV-2 Creation Wizard opens by default.</p><p>To create an AcV-2 diagram from the wizard</p><hr /><ol><li>From the <strong>Diagrams</strong> menu, select <strong>Create Diagram</strong>.</li><li>Click <strong>AcV-2 Project Timeliness</strong>.</li><li>In the <strong>AcV-2 Project Timeliness</strong> dialog, click <strong>Add</strong>. <strong>AcV-2 Creation Wizard</strong> opens.</li><li>Specify the diagram name and create or select the owner for the diagram.
